---
title: "Vignette - Virtual Environments"
id: vignette_virtual_environments
author: brad_cannell
date: 2026-01-31
date-modified: 2026-01-31
audience: developers
category: vignette
tags:
  - vs_code
  - positron
  - python
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    embed-resources: true
execute:
  eval: false
---

# Overview

- This vignette is about creating Python virtual environments (in Positron).
- 🔴 Add something about the using virtual environments across computers. See: https://chatgpt.com/c/697cebc4-e8a4-832b-a672-8e106b2134cb

# Overview

This vignette provides information about using Python and Chatlas](https://posit-dev.github.io/chatlas/) to interact with AI assistants.

# Create a virtual environment (venv)

Notes from [ChatGPT](https://chatgpt.com/g/g-p-694c5854fc0481918223d2c7988b756b-openai-api-python/c/6974e134-06e8-8329-a32c-249b49fecdef)

When Positron suggests creating a virtual environment (venv), it’s saying:

> “Let this project have its own Python and package setup, so changes here don’t accidentally break other projects—or vice versa.”

A venv is just:
- A **copy of the Python interpreter**
- Plus a private site-packages/ folder
- Scoped to one project
Nothing magical—just isolation.

## Why this matters (especially for a chat / API project)

If you install packages globally:

````bash
pip install openai pydantic rich
````

Those versions become:
- Shared by _every_ Python script on your machine
- Easy to accidentally upgrade/downgrade
- Hard to reproduce later

This becomes painful when:
- One project wants `pydantic<2`
- Another wants `pydantic>=2`
- Something silently breaks 😬

A `venv` prevents this class of problems entirely.

## Set up venv

### Method 1: Using the Command Palette (Recommended)
This is the fastest way to have Positron create and automatically register the environment for your current workspace.
1. **Open your project folder** in Positron.
2. Open the **Command Palette** by pressing `Ctrl+Shift+P` (Windows/Linux) or `Cmd+Shift+P` (macOS).
3. Type and select `Python: Create Environment....`
4. Select **venv** from the list of environment providers.
5. Choose the Python interpreter version you wish to use.
6. Positron will create a `.venv` folder in your project root and automatically set it as the active interpreter for your workspace. 

### Method 2: Manual Terminal Setup
If you prefer the command line or need specific configurations, you can create it manually. 
1. Open the integrated terminal in Positron.
2. Create the venv:
    - Windows: `python -m venv .venv`
    - macOS/Linux: `python3 -m venv .venv`
3. Activate the venv:
    - Windows: `.venv\Scripts\activate`
    - macOS/Linux: `source .venv/bin/activate`
4. Connect to Positron: Once created, click the Python Interpreter version in the top-right corner (or use Python: Select Interpreter in the Command Palette) and select the path to the new .venv to ensure the IDE uses it for linting and execution. 

## File paths

Your file paths aren’t actually more complicated (they just look that way)

The confusion usually comes from **activating** the venv, not from using it.

Once activated:

```bash
source .venv/bin/activate
```

Then:
- `python` → uses the venv Python
- `pip` → installs into the venv
- `sys.path` → resolves packages automatically

Your code does **not** need to reference `.venv` at all.

If you’re seeing paths like: `/Users/.../project/.venv/lib/python3.12/site-packages`

That’s just Python being transparent—not something you should ever type manually.

Do this once per project (Positron folder) and forget about it:

```bash
pwd
cd your-project
python -m venv .venv
source .venv/bin/activate
```

Then in Positron:
- Select the `.venv` Python interpreter
- Stop thinking about it
You’ll only notice it again when things don’t break.
